coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and modulation of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots from p-phenylenediamine and dansyl derivative for white light emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Davi, L.B.O.
Lima, D.J.P.
Barbosa, C.D.A.E.S.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) with multicolor emission are promising in the development of white light emitting diode (WLED) devices with high efficiency and low cost. In this study, we synthesized C-dots with tricolor emissions of blue (C-DsCl), green (C-pPDDS) and red (C-pPD) via the hydrothermal method using dansyl chloride, p-phenyldiaminadansyl and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, respectively. The fluorescent properties of the synthesized C-dots were found to be dependent on the presence of heteroatoms obtained via modulation of the p-phenylenediamine and dansyl chloride molecules, leading to an increased quantum yield for the N and S co-doped C-dots. The C-dots/PVP film displayed good CRI up to 89, CCT of 5628 K and color coordinates of (0.33, 0.31), close to that of pure white-light emission (0.33, 0.33). So, the C-dots with multicolor emission produced in this study can be used as a color converter to produce high-quality WLEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Dansyl
Tricolor emission
Device
WLEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots, C-pPDDS, C-pPD and C-DsCl
Preparation of emissive film and WLEDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior
Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa do Estado de Alagoas
Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to CNPq , FAPEAL (proc. 600301033/2016 ) for funding this work and CAPES for the PhD scholarship (proc. 88882.452008/2019-01 ). The authors thank the Optics and Nanoscopy Group (GON-IF-UFAL) for the optical measurements and Catalysis and Chemical Reactivity Group (GcaR-IQB-UFAL) to perform characterizations of materials. The authors would like to thank T. Oliveira dos Santos and Laboratório Multiusuário de Microscopia de Alta Resolução (LabMic) of University of Goiás, Brazil, for HRTEM measurements.

The authors are grateful to CNPq, FAPEAL (proc. 600301033/2016) for funding this work and CAPES for the PhD scholarship (proc. 88882.452008/2019-01). The authors thank the Optics and Nanoscopy Group (GON-IF-UFAL) for the optical measurements and Catalysis and Chemical Reactivity Group (GcaR-IQB-UFAL) to perform characterizations of materials. The authors would like to thank T. Oliveira dos Santos and Laboratório Multiusuário de Microscopia de Alta Resolução (LabMic) of University of Goiás, Brazil, for HRTEM measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of pPDDS, C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS by hydrothermal treatment and (b) images of C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS under visible light (left) and under UV irradiation (365 nm) (right).
TEM images of the C-dots: C-pPD (a–b), C-DsCl (c, d) and C-pPDDS (e–f).
FTIR spectra for C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS.
Absorption spectra for C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS.
Photostability for C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS irradiated by a 350 nm at various time.
Emission spectrum of the C-dots and corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram: C-pPD (a-b), C-DsCl (c, d) and C-pPDDS (e-f).
Graph showing λexc versus λem values for the C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS.
Spectrum of C-dots/PVP films and corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram: a) Exc. 370 nm and b) Exc. 390 nm.
Photographs of WLED devices at a) 370 nm and b) 390 nm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and modulation of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots from p-phenylenediamine and dansyl derivative for white light emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry and Biotechnology, Federal University of Alagoas, Campus A.C. Simões, Tabuleiro Dos Martins, Maceió, Alagoas, 57072-970, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry and Biotechnology, Federal University of Alagoas, Campus A.C. Simoes, Tabuleiro Dos Martins, Maceio, Alagoas, 57072-970, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) with multicolor emission are promising in the development of white light emitting diode (WLED) devices with high efficiency and low cost. In this study, we synthesized C-dots with tricolor emissions of blue (C-DsCl), green (C-pPDDS) and red (C-pPD) via the hydrothermal method using dansyl chloride, p-phenyldiaminadansyl and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, respectively. The fluorescent properties of the synthesized C-dots were found to be dependent on the presence of heteroatoms obtained via modulation of the p-phenylenediamine and dansyl chloride molecules, leading to an increased quantum yield for the N and S co-doped C-dots. The C-dots/PVP film displayed good CRI up to 89, CCT of 5628 K and color coordinates of (0.33, 0.31), close to that of pure white-light emission (0.33, 0.33). So, the C-dots with multicolor emission produced in this study can be used as a color converter to produce high-quality WLEDs.

ce:section.ce:para: White light emitting diodes (WLEDs) have been receiving attention due to their notable energy conservation capacity, which means they are both eco-friendly and have a long life [1]. Nevertheless, although these devices present high light conversion, the materials traditionally used to manufacture them, such as rare earth phosphorous and quantum semiconductor dots (QDs), are usually expensive, scarce and non-renewable. Also, these materials are associated with numerous adverse environmental impacts and present a high risk to human healt [2–4]. Thus, it is important to search for new materials with high efficiency, that are available in abundance and have biocompatibility properties, such as carbon dots (C-dots), which are fluorescent carbon nanoparticles that were accidently discovered by Xu et al., in 2004 [1–6].

C-dots are suitable for emission in the visible range of 400–800 nm, with high luminance and excellent photostability and low toxicity. Also, they are obtained from an abundant raw material. Therefore, they have great potential for use in the fabrication of color LEDs and WLEDs [7] [–] [9]. In general, C-dots are applied in the construction of these devices in two ways: C-dots with yellow emission in blue emission chips [2,10] or a combination of blue, yellow and red C-dots, in ideal proportions, to obtain the RGB system supported in LED chips, leading to white light emission [11–13]. The first case poses a problem related to the stable correlated color temperature (CCT), due to the different currents of blue chips and color purity in the absence of orange-red, while a drawback associated with the second case is the difficulty associated with synthesizing C-dots with red emission and with high quantum yield (QY) [2,10,14].

In the literature, several studies on the synthesis of C-dots with red emission using p-phenylenediamine as a precursor have been reported [15–19]. However, the red emission C-dots obtained have low QY. It is possible to supply the low QY of the red emitter with the other green and blue emitters of high QYs, but another issue is that red emission C-dots are obtained using acids, organic solvents and onerous purification procedures. Thus, they are associated with environmental damage and are laborious to produce [18,19]. This demonstrates the importance of the need for an approach to C-dots synthesis (mainly with red emission) where milder conditions can be applied to give high quantum yield.

The luminescence properties and QY of C-dots are influenced by several factors, such as the use of different reagents/molecular dopants, synthetic parameters and solvents [9,11,20–23]. To improve these properties a strategy commonly applied is the addition of dopants or molecules with several heteroatoms, since these allow the formation of different emissive states [24,25]. Yu and collaborators [26] reported C-dots with high quantum yield (QY) (73%) using nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) as dopants, which were obtained from citric acid (CA) and l-cysteine as sources of N and S. Similarly, Ding et al., 2018 [24] produced C-dots with blue emission and, through doping involving N and S, it was possible to obtain C-dots with high fluorescence efficiency. Dansyl chloride (DsCl) is an organic compound with N, S, O and Cl present in its composition, which is an attractive precursor of C-dots, because it allows the addition of different surface states, due to its compositional diversity [26–29].

Herein, we present a ‘green’ and facile route for obtaining multi-colored emission C-dots by the one-pot hydrothermal method using p-phenylenediamine, dansyl chloride and p-phenyldiaminadansyl (pPDDS) for the development of WLEDs.

All experimental procedures were performed using analytical grade reagents. Aqueous solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water (specific resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm and electrical conductivity <0.1 μS cm−1) from a MilliQ® water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). All chemical reagents used in the analytical procedures were of analytical grade. Chloroform, benzene and methanol from Merck (Darmstadt, Hessen, Germany) were used as solvents without further purification. The reagents 5-(dimethylamino)naphthalene-1-sulfonyl chloride (dansyl chloride), p-phenylenediamine, trimethylamine and MgSO4 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

A magnetically stirred solution of dansyl chloride (107.9 mg, 0.4 mmol) and p-phenylenediamine (43.5 mg, 0.4 mmol) in chloroform (10 mL) was added dropwise to trimethylamine (0.1 mL, 0.4 mmol). The reaction mixture thus obtained was heated to 80 °C and stirred at this temperature for 7 h. The mixture was quenched with water, extracted with ethyl acetate (3 × 10 mL) and the combined organic layer was dried with MgSO4, filtered and concentrated under reduce pressure. The crude residue was purified by chromatography on SiO2 (7:3, hexane/ethyl acetate) to obtain the desired compound (132 mg, 0.4 mmol, 97%) as a yellow solid: Mp. 82–89 °C; IR 783, 912, 1134, 1305, 1456, 1510, 1577, 1616, 2781, 2839, 2922, 3238, 3331, 3412 cm−1; 1H NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz) δ 2.89 (s, 6H), 6.40 (d, 2H, J = 8.6 Hz), 6.63 (d, 2H, J = 8.6 Hz), 7.20 (d, 1H, J = 7.5 Hz), 7.38 (dd, 1H, J = 7.5, 8.4 Hz), 7.58 (dd, 1H, J = 7.7, 8.5 Hz), 8.03 (dd, 1H, J = 1.2, 7.3 Hz), 8.34 (d, 1H, J = 8.7 Hz), 8.48 (d, 1H, J = 8.5 Hz). 13C NMR (CDCl3, 100 MHz) δ 45.4, 115.1, 118.3, 118.7, 122.8, 123.1, 126.4, 126.5, 128.4, 128.5, 129.7, 130.4, 130.8, 134.2, 145.1, 152.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots, C-pPDDS, C-pPD and C-DsCl
ce:section.ce:para: In a Teflon® reactor, pPDDS (10 mg, 0.029 mmol) was mixed with 10 mL of H2O. The reactor was maintained at 200 °C in a kiln for 4 h. The C-dots thus obtained were centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. C-dots were also synthesized from DsCl (10 mg, 0.032 mmol) and pPD (21.62 mg, 0.199 mmol) using the above-described procedure. The solutions obtained, with a reddish yellow color, containing the nanoparticles were stored at 4 °C for further analysis. The C-dots synthesized in water from pPDDS, pPD and DsCl were designated as C-pPDDS, C-pPD and C-DsCl, respectively. WLE-C-dots were prepared by mixing 1200 μL of C-pPD, 28 μL of C-DsCl and 15 μL of C-pPDDS with 3 mL of water. The produced C-dots stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C proved to be stable for more than six months without any color change or precipitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of emissive film and WLEDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emissive films were prepared with a solution containing 300 mg polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), 1800 μL C-pPD, 15 μL C-DsCl and 15 μL C-pPDDS in 800 μL of water and constantly stirred to form a homogeneous solution. The mixture was placed on a glass plate and dried for 3 h at 60 °C. It was then coated onto the surface of a LED chip (390 nm). CIE chromaticity coordinates were obtained using the CIE-1931 color space of an software ColorCalculator (Osram Sylvania, MA, EUA).

Visualization by thin layer chromatography (TLC) was achieved with the use of UV light (254 nm). The melting points were determined by means of a Kofler melting point microscope. 1H NMR (400 MHz) and 13C (100 MHz) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance spectrometer, using CDCl3 solution with trimethylsilane (TMS) as the internal standard. IR spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu IR PRESTIGE-21 FTIR spectrophotometer. UV–Vis spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (1 cm quartz cell). The photoluminescence properties were investigated using a FluoroLog®-3 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp (CW 450 W) as an excitation source and a photomultiplier detector (model R928P). All optical data were obtained using sample solutions at room temperature (273 K). Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) images of the multicolored-emission C-dots were obtained with a transmission electron microscope (JEOL model JEM 2100, Tokyo, Japan) operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV.

The fluorescence quantum yields (FLQYs) of the multicolored-emission C-dots were measured using the following reference standards: quinine sulfate (QY = 55% in 0.1 M H2SO4) for C-DsCl, rhodamine 6 G (QY = 95% in absolute ethanol) for C-pPDDS and rhodamine B (QY = 56% in absolute ethanol) for C-pPD. The FLQYs of the multicolored-emission C-dots were calculated by the equation:
Ф x
=
Ф R
(
a x
a R
)
(
η x 2
η R 2
)
where Фx and Ф R are the FLQYs of the C-dots and standard, respectively, a is the slope determined from the curves, η is the refractive index (1.33 in water and 1.36 in ethanol). The subscripts x and R represent the parameters of the experiment for C-dots and the standard, respectively.

The p-phenyldiaminadansyl (pPDDS) was obtained from the acyclic nucleophilic substitution reaction between p-phenylenediamine and dansyl chloride in the presence of triethylamine in chloroform which, after purification by chromatographic column, led to pPDDS in 97% yield. After characterization, pPDDS and its precursors were used to synthesize the C-dots through hydrothermal reaction via the bottom-up route (Fig. 1 (a)). The C-dots showed blue, green and red fluorescence emission under UV light (365 nm), for C-DsCl, C-pPDDS and C-pPD, respectively (Fig. 1(b)).

The morphological and size data for the C-dots were obtained from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2 (a–f)). The mean diameters were well below 10 nm, with values of 3.32 ± 0.40, 4.33 ± 0.53 and 1.97 ± 0.31 nm for C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS, respectively, which is in agreement with data published in the literature [23,24,30]. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images demonstrate a well resolved crystalline network with interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the graphene plane pattern spacing (100 bevel) and the graphene layer spacing (002 bevel), respectively (Fig. 2(b), (d) and 2(f)) [31,32].

The optical properties of the synthesized C-dots were investigated by absorption (UV–Vis) and emission (fluorescence) spectroscopy techniques. C-dots typically exhibit peaks in two characteristic regions in the UV–vis spectrum, one between 220 and 280 nm, assigned to the π-π* transitions (of the sp2 hybridized graphite core) and the other between 280 and 580 nm related to the n-π* transitions (associated with functional surface groups) (Fig. 4 ) [34,35].

In the C-dot spectrum obtained for pPD it is possible to observe bands centered at 301 and 513 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* transitions characteristics of C–N and CN groups [36]. In the case of C-DsCl, there are four absorption bands, two of them (217 and 240 nm) in the region of π-π* and two related to the n-π* transitions (294 and 306 nm), which are evidence of compounds containing sulfur in their structure [34,35]. Analysis of the C-pPDDS spectrum shows a broader band, in the π-π* and n-π* transition regions, indicating the presence of nitrogen and sulfur in its structure. This differs from C-DsCl, which has a band at 518 nm, associated with C–N and CN bonds, similarly to C-pPD, but shifted to lower energies due to the presence of sulfur in the structure [11,25,37], in agreement with the FTIR data.

The FLQYs determined for C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS C-dots were 0.63%, 11.37% and 13.25%, respectively. These results indicate that the doping of N and S in the synthesis of the pPDDS molecule via the reaction between pPD and DsCl provided an increase in the FLQY values for the C-pPDDS. Although the p-phenylenediamine-derived C-dots showed a very low FLQY when compared with other works in the literature, in this study we chose to use green methodologies free of acids and potentially toxic solvents [17,33,38–41]. In addition, the synthesis of C-dots from p-phenylenediamine via hydrothermal methods usually have very low quantum yields below 3% [42]. The photostability of C-dots was performed under continuous excitation at 350 nm for a period of 60 min and no significant change was found in fluorescence intensity suggesting that the C-dots are photostable (Fig. 5 ).

In addition, the emission spectra for the C-dots showed distinct optical behavior with respect to the spectral profile at different excitation wavelengths (λexc), which ranged from 300 to 540 nm for C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS (Fig. 6 ((a) – (f)). The C-pPDs showed an excitation-independent emission spectrum, with maximum emission at 628 nm (λexc = 360 nm) (Fig. 6 ((a)–(b)). The emission of C-DsCl had a maximum at 417 nm (λexc = 390 nm), with a broadband and an excitation-dependent emission spectrum (Fig. 6 (c)–(d))). On the other hand, the C-dots obtained from the pPDDS display a maximum emission at 497 nm (λexc = 360 nm) and an excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 6 (e)–(f)), although less variable than that of the C-DsCl, suggesting that the combination of the characteristics of its percussors containing N and S allowed the formation of two well-defined states centered at 425–625 nm.

In order to better elucidate the dependence and independence of the C-dots, the data are presented in a graph of λexc versus λem (Fig. 7 ). An analysis of the C-pPD shows behavior independent of the excitation wavelength, with the emission being practically unaltered (around 630 nm). On the other hand, when observing the emission of C-DsCl, dependence on λexc is evident, with a shift to the red region of the spectrum with increasing λexc, and four emission levels are presented (417, 502, 550 and 605 nm). In addition, C-pPDDS was obtained from pPDDS, in which the pPD containing only nitrogen in its structure received doping with sulfur through coupling with dansyl chloride (DsCl). As expected, doping with sulfur influenced the structural and optical properties of the C-dots obtained. In the literature, it is proposed that surface defects lead to different emission states, resulting in a dependence on the excitation wavelength [29,43–45]. In view of this, on comparing C-pPDDS with C-pPD, it can be verified that doping with sulfur leads to an increase in surface defects, resulting in two emission states (at 496 and 590 nm).

The luminescence properties of C-dots were explored for modulation of the white light emission via the RGB system, based on the different emission contributions of the C-pPD, C-DsCl and C-pPDDS C-dots. Considering the white emission of the C-dots solution with ideal proportions, white light emitting (WLE) films were produced with mixed C-dots, using a PVP aqueous solution to construct a polymeric film. The C-dots/PVP films prepared were pale yellow in daylight and when excited at 370 and 390 nm displayed a WLE with CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.31), CCT 5628 K, CRI 79 and (0.28, 0.33), CCT 8172 K, CRI 89 respectively, which is very close to those of pure white light (0.33, 0.33), (Fig. 8 (a) – (d)) [11,13,46,47].
In order to obtain WLEDs, the C-dots/PVP films were supported on commercial 370 and 390 nm LED chips and presented an impressive white light emission (Fig. 9 ).

Fluorescence blue, green and red carbon dots were synthesized by the hydrothermal method from dansyl chloride, p-phenyldiaminadansyl and p-phenylenediamine. The quantum yield increased to up to 13.25% for N–S co-doped carbon dots. The films produced from the multicolor carbon dots in PVP exhibited CRI up to 89 and CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.31) and (0.28, 0.33) under UV excitation of 370 and 390 nm, respectively. In addition, WLEDs produced by supporting C-dots/PVP films in 370 and 390 nm LED chips displayed impressive white light emission. To the best of our knowledge, this is a unique mixture of C-dots from dansyl, p-phenylenediamine and p-phenyldiaminadansyl that when combined in polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) films and supported on an ultraviolet-excited LED device produced WLE.

L.B.O. Davi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. D.J.P. Lima: Investigation, Writing, Supervision. C.D.A.E.S. Barbosa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to CNPq, FAPEAL (proc. 600301033/2016) for funding this work and CAPES for the PhD scholarship (proc. 88882.452008/2019-01). The authors thank the Optics and Nanoscopy Group (GON-IF-UFAL) for the optical measurements and Catalysis and Chemical Reactivity Group (GcaR-IQB-UFAL) to perform characterizations of materials. The authors would like to thank T. Oliveira dos Santos and Laboratório Multiusuário de Microscopia de Alta Resolução (LabMic) of University of Goiás, Brazil, for HRTEM measurements.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red carbon dots-based phosphors for white light-emitting diodes with color rendering index of 92
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

Nanomaterials

Adv. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots-based flexible films and their application as the phosphor in white light-emitting diodes
Chem. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright multicolor bandgap fluorescent carbon quantum dots for electroluminescent light-emitting diodes
Adv. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Extremely high color rendering white light from surface passivated carbon dots and Zn-doped AgInS2 nanocrystals
J. Mater. Chem. C.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White-light emission of single carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal carbonization of poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride): applications to fabrication of white-light-emitting films
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

Colloid Interface Sci. 556 (2019) 120-127. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.08.049.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of single-particle level white-light-emitting carbon dots via a one-step microwave method
J. Mater. Chem. C.
